---
title: "White Flight and the Persistence of Residential Segregation in Mobile County, AL"
author: 
  - name: Ethan Gwinn
    affil: 1, 2
  - name: Nathan Alexander, PhD
    affil: 3, 4

affiliation:
  - num: 1
    address: "Department of Sociology"
  - num: 2
    address: "Department of Economics"
  - num: 3
    address: "Department of Curriculum and Instruction"
  - num: 4
    address: "Center for Applied Data Science and Analytics (CADSA)"

column_numbers: 4

logoright_name: "img/qs-logo.png"
logoleft_name: "img/hu-logo.jpg"

titlebox_borderwidth: "0.25cm"

primary_colour: "#ffffff"
secondary_colour: "#5F6062"
accent_colour: "#E51937"

titlebox_bordercol: "#003A63"
title_textcol: "#003A63"
author_textcol: "#003A63"
affiliation_textcol: "#003A63"

columnline_col: "#E51937"
columnline_style: solid

sectitle_textcol: "#ffffff"
sectitle_bgcol: "#003A63"
sectitle_bordercol: "#003A63"
sectitle2_textcol: "#003A63"

output: 
  posterdown::posterdown_html:
    css: hu-poster.css

bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
 # cache = FALSE,
  fig.width = 3,
  fig.height = 2,
  dpi = 300
)

# Ensure required packages are installed and loaded
if (!requireNamespace("posterdown", quietly = TRUE)) {
  pak::pak("brentthorne/posterdown")
}
if (!requireNamespace("pagedown", quietly = TRUE)) {
  pak::pak("rstudio/pagedown")
}

library(posterdown)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(here)
here::i_am("howard-template.Rmd") # if you change the name of your file, update this
```

<style>
@page {
size: 48in 36in;
margin: 2in;
}
</style>

```{r, eval=F, include=FALSE}
# To render both HTML and PDF versions:
rmarkdown::render("howard-template.Rmd")

pagedown::chrome_print(
  "howard-template.html",
  output = "howard-template.pdf",
  options = list(
    paperWidth = 48, 
    paperHeight = 36
    )
)
```

# Abstract

This study analyzes demographic shifts in Mobile County from 1950 to the present to assess the persistence of residential segregation. By utilizing decennial census data and spatial analysis, the research examines how structural and historical forces continue to drive spatial inequality in the post–civil rights era.

# Overview

White flight occurs when white individuals abandon integrated spaces to avoid growing minority populations and stall racial progress. This migration is driven by the desire to protect "whiteness as property," where distancing themselves from other races preserves their social and economic capital. (Crowder & South, 2008) In Mobile, desegregation served interest convergence: civil rights concessions were only made when they protected the elite’s economic interests and established new, spatial forms of white exclusivity. (Bell, 1980)

Segregation Facilitators

Whiteness as property: Whiteness is not just as a racial identity, but as a legal and social asset. It posits that European ancestry carries a distinct set of inherent privileges, sociopolitical status, and expectations of entitlement that function as a protected form of property. (Donner, 2021)

Interest Convergence: Racial equality is only achieved when the interests of Black people align with the self-interests of the white majority.  (Bell, 1980)


# Literature Review

Between 1970-1980, Mobile County saw a significant decline in public school enrollment (approx. 11,500 students), directly correlating with a rise in private school attendance and suburban migration (Pride, 2022). This period highlights a distinct pattern of "white flight" from urban centers to suburban districts.

White residents often undervalue racially integrated areas due to an "aversion to living near black neighbors" and inflated perceptions of crime (Crowder & South, 2008). These subjective assessments frequently override objective neighborhood quality, driving residential turnover.



# Research Question

1. How have Black and white racial demographics across census tracts in Mobile County evolved from 1950-2020?

H1: We hypothesize that the index of dissimilarity will show relatively little change (non-significant difference) over the time period examined due to external factors such as urban development, displacement, and gentrification.


# Methods and Data

This is some sample text. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio.

This is some more sample text. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam.

# Findings

Here is finding 1. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam.

Here is finding 2. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam.

```{r, include=F, warning=F, messages=F}
# add your data here; note that the code and output are hidden (i.e., include =F)

# Create a data frame for the top 10 most populous countries
countries <- c("India", "China", "United States", "Indonesia", 
               "Pakistan", "Nigeria", "Brazil", "Bangladesh", 
               "Russia", "Mexico")

populations <- c(1463870000, 1416100000, 347276000, 285721000, 
                 255220000, 237528000, 212812000, 175687000, 
                 147190000, 128271248)

# Combine into a data frame
population_data <- data.frame(Country = countries, Population = populations)
```

```{r, echo=F, warning=F, messages=F}
# here we will hide the code put include a plot of the data

# Create a bar plot using ggplot2
ggplot(population_data, aes(x = reorder(Country, -Population), y = Population)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Most Populous Countries (2025)", 
       x = "Countries", y = "Population") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

```

Here is finding 3. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam.

# Discussion

Here is a sample citation from the references.bib file. [@alexander2021new]. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam.

# Conclusion

This is some concluding text. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam.

# Acknowledgements

These are the acknowledgements. This work was supported by funding from the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation (Grant 2023-21062). Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Integer nec odio. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam.

# References
